sections.header: Verses 1-6 — The Image of Jealousy at the Gate

heb.paragraph: The 'image of jealousy' (semel haqqin'ah) stood at the north gate of the temple — an idol placed in the very entrance of God's house. The word qin'ah ('jealousy') echoes the divine attribute: YHWH is a jealous God (Exod 20:5). The idol provokes the jealousy of the One whose house it desecrates.

refs.note: Manasseh placed an Asherah pole in the temple — a likely historical precedent for the image of jealousy.
The second commandment prohibits images and declares YHWH a jealous God — the very jealousy this idol provokes.

notes.note: The date formula places this vision in September 592 BC, fourteen months after the inaugural vision. The elders of Judah sitting before Ezekiel suggests his growing role as spiritual authority among the exiles.

The Spirit lifts Ezekiel by a lock of his hair — a vivid, physical description of visionary transport. He sees the glory of God still present in the temple, making the idolatry all the more outrageous.
The idol at the north gate is designed to drive God away from his sanctuary. Idolatry does not merely offend God; it forces a departure.

Calvin notes that the vision is given while the elders sit before Ezekiel — God reveals Jerusalem's corruption to the exiles so they understand why the city must fall. The vision serves a pastoral purpose: removing false hope. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The figure 'like a man' (demut kemar'eh ish) uses the same approximation language as 1:26 — this is likely the same divine figure from the throne vision, now acting as tour guide.
Visionary transport 'between earth and heaven' places Ezekiel in a liminal state — his body remains in Babylon while his spirit sees Jerusalem.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies the four-scene structure as a guided tour moving progressively deeper into the temple complex — from the outer gate (v.5) to the inner court (v.16). Each scene reveals worse abominations, building the theological case for God's departure. The structure mirrors a legal prosecution.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli argues the 'image of jealousy' is not necessarily Asherah but could be any cultic image. The point is not the identity of the idol but its location: it stands where only YHWH should be honored. Placement, not type, constitutes the offense. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: Fourteen months after his inaugural vision (September 592 BC), Ezekiel is transported in a vision to the Jerusalem temple. What follows is a guided tour of abominations — four escalating scenes of idolatry, each deeper within the sacred precincts. The vision answers the question: why will God abandon his temple? Because Israel has already abandoned him within it.

sections.header: Verses 7-14 — Creeping Things, Elders in Darkness, and Weeping for Tammuz

heb.paragraph: Tammuz was a Mesopotamian fertility god whose annual death and descent to the underworld was ritually mourned. Women weeping for Tammuz at the temple gate imported Babylonian religion into the heart of YHWH worship. The irony is devastating: exiles in Babylon are watching Jerusalem adopt the very religion of their captors.

refs.note: They exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images resembling mortal creatures — Paul's language echoes Ezekiel's animal-worship scene.
Judean women worshiped the Queen of Heaven — parallel evidence of fertility cult worship among Judeans.

notes.note: The wall carvings of detestable animals represent Egyptian-style worship — Israel borrowed from every surrounding culture. The hole in the wall suggests secrecy: they thought God could not see.
Seventy elders — the number echoes the seventy elders of Exodus 24:1 who saw God on Sinai. The leaders who should have upheld covenant loyalty lead the apostasy.
Their theology justifies the sin: 'The LORD does not see us; the LORD has forsaken the land.' They deny divine omniscience and providence simultaneously.
Women weeping for Tammuz represents the adoption of Babylonian mourning rituals — the very culture that conquered them now conquers their worship.

Calvin highlights the self-deception: the elders claim God has abandoned the land, so they are free to worship other gods. In reality, their idolatry is what will cause God to leave — they confuse cause and effect. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The 'creeping things and loathsome beasts' (remes ubehemah) use the language of Genesis 1 — creation vocabulary applied to idolatrous images, suggesting a parody of the created order.
Jaazaniah son of Shaphan may be the brother of Ahikam who protected Jeremiah (Jer 26:24) — a prominent family divided between faithfulness and apostasy.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block reads the progression as deliberately shocking: each scene is introduced with 'you will see still greater abominations.' The rhetoric of escalation builds the case that Jerusalem's corruption is not accidental but comprehensive — every stratum of society participates: elders, women, priests.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli connects the Tammuz weeping to the broader pattern of syncretism: Israel does not replace YHWH worship but supplements it with foreign cults. This additive syncretism is more dangerous than outright apostasy because it maintains the appearance of loyalty while hollowing out the substance.

hist.context: The tour reveals three escalating abominations: elders worshiping animal images in secret chambers (Egyptian-style theriomorphic cult), women weeping for Tammuz (Mesopotamian fertility rites), and men worshiping the sun (astral cult). Israel has become a religious marketplace, importing every form of paganism while maintaining the outward shell of YHWH worship. The seventy elders represent the leadership class — the corruption is systemic, not marginal.

sections.header: Verses 15-18 — Worshiping the Sun, Backs to the Temple

heb.paragraph: Twenty-five men stand between the porch and the altar — the most sacred zone of the temple — with their backs to the temple and their faces toward the east, worshiping the rising sun. The physical posture captures the spiritual reality: Israel has turned its back on YHWH. This is the climactic abomination.

refs.note: Josiah removed the priests who burned incense to the sun, moon, and stars — the same astral cult resurfaces after his death.
Moses warned against being enticed to worship the sun, moon, and stars — the very sin the priests now commit.

notes.note: Between the portico and the altar — this is the most sacred zone where only priests should be, performing authorized worship. Instead, they worship the sun. The location intensifies the sacrilege.
Putting the branch to the nose may be a pagan ritual gesture or a crude euphemism for an obscene act. Either way, it compounds the insult to God.
God's response: no pity, no compassion. Even if they cry out, he will not listen. The covenant of mercy has been exhausted by persistent, escalating rebellion.

Calvin sees the priests turning their backs on the temple as the most powerful image in the chapter: those ordained to face God have deliberately turned away. Their physical posture enacts their spiritual apostasy. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The twenty-five men likely represent the twenty-four priestly courses (1 Chr 24:1-19) plus the high priest — the entire priestly establishment is implicated.

The phrase 'putting the branch to their nose' (zemorah el appam) is obscure. It may be a euphemism altered by scribes (the Masoretic tradition notes this as a tiqqun sopherim — a scribal correction to avoid irreverence).

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block emphasises the spatial symbolism: the priests stand exactly where they should — between porch and altar — but face the wrong direction. They occupy the correct position with the wrong orientation. This is Ezekiel's image for institutional religion that retains form while losing substance.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies the sun worship as the logical climax: the temple tour moves from external gate to inner court, from laity to leadership, from imported cult to cosmic idolatry. The progression is both spatial and theological — each step penetrates deeper into the sacred and deeper into apostasy.

hist.context: The fourth and worst scene: twenty-five men (probably twenty-four priestly divisions plus the high priest) worship the sun in the inner court, backs turned to the Holy of Holies. Sun worship was the dominant imperial religion of the ancient Near East. By adopting it in the temple's most sacred space, the priests signal ultimate betrayal — they have chosen the religion of empire over the God who delivered them from empire.

rows.label: vv.14-18
rows.text: Scene 4: Sun Worship — Backs to God

lit.note: A guided tour of four escalating abominations, moving from outer gate to inner court. Each scene is worse than the last, building the case for God's departure from the temple.

themes.note: Worship peaks at 10: the chapter is entirely about false worship in God's own temple. Holiness at 9: the desecration of sacred space drives the entire narrative.

hebtext.note: The 'image of jealousy' (semel haqqin'ah) stood at the north gate of the temple — an idol placed in the very entrance of God's house. The word qin'ah ('jealousy') echoes the divine attribute: YHWH is a jealous God (Exod 20:5). The idol provokes the jealousy of the One whose house it desecrates.

Tammuz was a Mesopotamian fertility god whose annual death and descent to the underworld was ritually mourned. Women weeping for Tammuz at the temple gate imported Babylonian religion into the heart of YHWH worship. The irony is devastating: exiles in Babylon are watching Jerusalem adopt the very religion of their captors.

Twenty-five men stand between the porch and the altar — the most sacred zone of the temple — with their backs to the temple and their faces toward the east, worshiping the rising sun. The physical posture captures the spiritual reality: Israel has turned its back on YHWH. This is the climactic abomination.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Abominations in the Temple

translations.text: Then he said to me, “Son of man, look toward the north.” So I looked, and in the entrance north of the gate of the altar I saw this idol of jealousy.
Then he said to me, “Son of man, lift up your eyes now toward the north.” So I lifted up my eyes toward the north, and behold, north of the altar gate, in the entrance, was this image of jealousy.
I looked, and I saw a figure like that of a man. From what appeared to be his waist down he was like fire, and from there up his appearance was as bright as glowing metal.

Then I looked, and behold, a form that had the appearance of a man. Below what appeared to be his waist was fire, and above his waist was something like the appearance of brightness, like gleaming metal.

thread.text: Manasseh placed an Asherah pole in the temple — a likely historical precedent for the image of jealousy.
The second commandment prohibits images and declares YHWH a jealous God — the very jealousy this idol provokes.
They exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images resembling mortal creatures — Paul's language echoes Ezekiel's animal-worship scene.
Judean women worshiped the Queen of Heaven — parallel evidence of fertility cult worship among Judeans.
Josiah removed the priests who burned incense to the sun, moon, and stars — the same astral cult resurfaces after his death.
Moses warned against being enticed to worship the sun, moon, and stars — the very sin the priests now commit.

coaching.tip: God's hand grabs Ezekiel by the hair and transports him in a vision to Jerusalem's temple (v. 3). The prophet sees what's happening 600 miles away while sitting in his house in Babylon. Ezekiel operates in two locations simultaneously — physical body in exile, prophetic sight in Jerusalem. This dual consciousness anticipates Paul's 'whether in the body or out of the body, I do not know' (2 Corinthians 12:2).

The temple tour reveals escalating abominations: an idol at the gate, animal worship in secret chambers, women weeping for Tammuz, men bowing to the sun with their backs to God's temple (vv. 5–16). Each chamber is worse than the last — 'you will see things that are even more detestable' (vv. 6, 13, 15). The literary structure of progressive revelation mirrors God's experience: each discovery deepens the offense.
